title: Methodological Framework
text: International Water Hyacinth Genomics Initiative

title: Methodological Framework
text:
Collaboration Model
This initiative is designed to be a distributed and inclusive consortium.

An open call for participation to research groups worldwide.
Clear guidelines on data ownership, authorship, and acknowledgment.
Opportunities for co-authored publications and thematic working groups.
Regular communication through virtual meetings, workshops, and shared online platforms.

Participation levels are flexible, enabling contributors to engage based on their resources and interests.
Beyond initial data generation, the initiative aims to:

Serve as a “living genomic resource”, continuously expanded with new samples and data.
Integrate genomic information with ecological, climatic, and distributional data.
Support training and capacity building, particularly in regions heavily affected by the invasion.
Provide a model framework applicable to other invasive species.

title: Methodological Framework
text:
Sampling Strategy
A coordinated sampling strategy will aim to cover:

The native range of the species.
Multiple invaded regions across continents.
Diverse environmental and ecological conditions (multiple habitats).

To ensure data comparability, contributors will be encouraged to follow standardized sampling and preservation protocols. They will also be provided with detailed guidelines for tissue collection, DNA extraction, and storage.

title: Methodological Framework
text:
Genomic Data Generation
The core genomic approach will be whole-genome sequencing (WGS) using either short- or long-read technologies. Depending on the available resources, the following sequencing strategies may be employed:

Resequencing of multiple individuals across populations.
Hybrid assemblies combining short and long reads.

title: Methodological Framework
text:
Data Management and Curation
All genomic data will be: - Stored in a centralized database with standardized formats.

Linked to comprehensive metadata records.
Submitted, when possible, to public repositories (e.g., international nucleotide sequence databases), following FAIR data principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable).

Quality control pipelines and documentation will be developed to ensure the reliability and reproducibility of the data.

title: Participation and Data Contribution
text:
To achieve broad geographic and ecological coverage, this project invites researchers to contribute plant tissue samples, extracted DNA, and/or whole-genome sequencing data. The project is designed to be flexible, allowing contributors to participate according to their available resources while adhering to shared standards that ensure data comparability and reproducibility.
Contributors may participate by:

sending plant tissues preserved in silica gel,
sending extracted genomic DNA, and/or
sharing whole-genome sequencing data generated independently.

title: Participation and Data Contribution
text:
Initial contact
Contact the coordinating team to express interest and indicate the type of contribution (plant tissue, DNA, and/or genomic data).

title: Participation and Data Contribution
text:
Sampling and preservation

Collect young, healthy leaf tissue when possible.
Preserve tissue immediately in silica gel until fully desiccated.
Clearly label each sample with a unique identifier.

title: Participation and Data Contribution
text:
Metadata recording
Record standardized metadata for each sample, including (at minimum): - Geographic coordinates and locality description - Habitat and environmental context - Sampling date and collector - Preservation method

title: Participation and Data Contribution
text:
DNA extraction or sequencing
Contributors may extract genomic DNA the provided guidelines, orPerform whole-genome sequencing locally using short- or long-read technologies.

title: Participation and Data Contribution
text:
Sample or data submission
Ship silica-dried tissues or extracted DNA provided instructions, or share raw sequencing reads and associated metadata through the designated data-transfer platform.

title: Participation and Data Contribution
text:
Data integration and acknowledgment
All contributions will be curated centrally, integrated into downstream analyses, and appropriately acknowledged through co-authorship or formal data citation, depending on the level of contribution.

title: International Water Hyacinth Genomics Initiative
text:
Objectives

Generate a global genomic database of Pontederia crassipes (Water Hyacinth) using whole-genome sequencing and complementary genomic approaches.
Characterize genetic diversity and population structure of P. crassipes across the native and invaded ranges.
Reconstruct invasion routes and dispersal pathways of P. crassipes at regional and global scales.
Identify genomic regions associated with adaptation of P. crassipes, invasiveness, and environmental tolerance.
Promote international collaboration and data sharing among P. crassipes researchers, institutions, and management agencies.

title: International Water Hyacinth Genomics Initiative
text:
Scope of the Initiative
The initiative is an open and collaborative framework designed to welcome contributions from researchers worldwide. Participation may include:

Providing plant tissue or DNA samples from well-documented populations.
Contributing raw whole-genome sequencing data or assembled genomes.
Sharing associated metadata, including geographic location, habitat type, sampling date, and ecological context.
Participating in joint analyses, publications, and related activities.

title: Guides and Protocols
text:
Our laboratory uses standardized protocols for the collection, extraction, and quantification of nucleic acids, specifically optimized for water hyacinth (Pontederia crassipes).





For optimal DNA preservation, tissue must be dried using silica gel immediately after collection in the field.



Scheme proposed by FuEDEI created with notebooklm.



Select healthy, young leaves of P. crassipes.
Cut small pieces of the leaf, avoiding thick, highly hydrated spongy tissues if possible.
Place the tissue pieces in a zip-lock bag filled with color-indicating silica gel.
Allow to dry completely (the tissue should become brittle and snap when bent).




A voucher specimen of each collected plant must be deposited in a recognized local herbarium. Additionally, high-quality photographs of the plant in its habitat and the pressed specimen must be archived by the collaborator.



A mounted Pontederia crassipes specimen serving as an example of proper herborization. Taken from GBIF.org. Complete reference: Teisher J, Stimmel H (2026). Tropicos MO Specimen Data. Missouri Botanical Garden. Occurrence dataset https://doi.org/10.15468/hja69f accessed via GBIF.org on 2026-05-27. https://www.gbif.org/occurrence/1259541625


Follow the specific herbarium protocols of your local institution. If no specific guidelines for aquatic plants are provided by your local herbarium, use the following method adapted for aquatic flora (Rodríguez & Rojas, 2006):

Aquatic Plant Pressing: Do not press directly into dry newspaper, as delicate aquatic parts (like Pontederia flowers) may adhere to the paper and be destroyed upon removal.
Collect the plants in wet newspaper and roll them up.
In the laboratory/herbarium, place the plant in a tray of water to allow all parts to spread out and extend naturally.
Slide a piece of white cardstock under the plant and carefully lift it out of the water, keeping the plant fixed to the cardstock.
Place the cardstock with the plant inside the pressing paper (newspaper/blotters) and proceed with standard drying methods.




Regardless of the specific label requirements of the local herbarium for the voucher specimen, all collaborators must record and retain the following metadata for every collected sample. This information is critical for our global database.
Please record the data using these exact fields:







Field Name
Description & Examples




code
Unique sample identifier (e.g., CAM0001, M04)


no_samples
Number of samples collected (e.g., 1, 5)


preservation
Tissue preservation method (e.g., Dry leaf in silica, Fresh leaf)


herbo
Herbarium code where the voucher is deposited


country
Country of collection (e.g., Argentina, Mexico)


stateProvince
State, province, or major region (e.g., Buenos Aires)


locality
Specific collection site (e.g., Otamendi, Laguna San Vicente)


decimalLatitude
Latitude in decimal degrees (e.g., 12.020736)


decimalLongitude
Longitude in decimal degrees (e.g., -61.762672)


elevation
Elevation in meters above sea level


waterBody
Name or type of the water body


Date (year/month/day)
Year, Month, and Day of collection


fieldNotes
Any relevant observations about the plant or environment


recordedBy
Name of the collector (e.g., A. Faltlhauser, A. Sosa)


scientificName
Pontederia crassipes


degreeOfEstablishment
Status in the area (e.g., Native, Invasive)










This protocol has been specifically tested and validated in our laboratory for water hyacinth. While the specific brand or model of the disruption equipment is flexible, the reagent modifications and vortexing parameters are crucial due to the plant’s high phytochemical content.
Starting Material & Pre-treatment: - Always start with silica-gel dried plant tissue. - Crucial step: Just prior to disruption, freeze the tissue using liquid nitrogen. If liquid nitrogen is unavailable, place the tissue in a -20°C or -80°C freezer overnight (or according to standard DNA preservation protocols).
Preparation and Homogenization: 1. Transfer the frozen tissue to your disruption tubes. 2. Lysis Buffer: Add the lysis buffer. (Note: The standard QIAGEN protocol uses 500 µl of Solution CD1. However, because P. crassipes is high in phenolic compounds, our in-house validated method requires adding 450 µl of Solution CD1 and 50 µl of Solution PS). 3. Homogenization: You may use any available disruption equipment in your laboratory (e.g., any brand of vortex with an adapter, bead beater, or tissue homogenizer). However, you must follow the established parameters to ensure complete pulverization: Vortex at maximum speed (between 10–15) for 3 to 5 minutes.
Extraction Steps: 4. Centrifuge the disruption tubes at 12,000 x g for 2 min. 5. Transfer the supernatant to a clean 1.5 ml tube. 6. Add 200 µl of Solution CD2 and vortex for 5 s. 7. Centrifuge at 12,000 x g for 1 min. Avoiding the pellet, transfer the supernatant to a clean 1.5 ml tube. 8. Add 500 µl of Buffer APP and vortex for 5 s. 9. Load 600 µl of lysate onto an MB Spin Column. Centrifuge at 12,000 x g for 1 min. Discard the flow-through (repeat if necessary to process all lysate). 10. Place the column into a clean 2 ml collection tube. Add 650 µl of Buffer AW1, centrifuge at 12,000 x g for 1 min, and discard the flow-through. 11. Add 650 µl of Buffer AW2, centrifuge at 12,000 x g for 1 min, and discard the flow-through. 12. Centrifuge the column at up to 16,000 x g for 2 min to completely dry the membrane. 13. Elution: Add 50–100 µl of Buffer EB directly to the center of the membrane, let it sit briefly at room temperature, then centrifuge at 12,000 x g for 1 min.





Collaborators may use any spectrophotometer or fluorometer available in their facilities. However, due to the high concentration of secondary metabolites in P. crassipes, we require specific criteria for determining quality and quantity.


Spectrophotometers measure absorbance and are highly susceptible to interference from phytochemicals, carbohydrates, and phenols common in water hyacinth. Therefore, this method should be used strictly to assess DNA quality, not absolute quantity. * A260/280 Ratio (Protein contamination): The accepted range for pure DNA is ~1.8 to 2.0. * A260/230 Ratio (Carbohydrate/Phenol contamination): The ideal accepted range is 1.8 – 2.2. In plants with high secondary metabolites, this value is often lower, but values significantly < 1.5 indicate contamination that may inhibit downstream PCR or NGS applications (Desjardins et al., 2009).



For accurate yield determination, fluorometric methods are required. Fluorometers use intercalating dyes that bind specifically to double-stranded DNA (dsDNA), bypassing the absorbance interference caused by plant secondary metabolites. * Always report the final DNA concentration (ng/µl) based on fluorometric readings to ensure an accurate input calculation for genomic sequencing.

title: Contact
text:
If you’d like to get in touch, feel free to reach us through any of the channels below.


📧 Email contact
waterhyacinthgenomics@gmail.com

title: Institutions
text:
Institutions

  



Coordinators

Dr. Alejandro Sosa - Fundación para el Estudio de Especies Invasivas (FuEDEI) & Consejo Nacional de Investigaciones Científicas y Técnicas (CONICET), Argentina.
Dr. Andrés F. Sánchez Restrepo - Fundación para el Estudio de Especies Invasivas (FuEDEI) & Universidad de Buenos Aires (UBA), Argentina.
Megan Reid, PhD - University of Florida Fort Lauderdale Research and Education Center, USA.
Melissa Smith, PhD - Invasive Plant Research Laboratory (USDA), USA.
Prof. Julie Coetzee - Deputy Director of the Centre for Biological Control at Rhodes University, South Africa.
Ana Faltlhauser - Fundación para el Estudio de Especies Invasivas (FuEDEI), Argentina.
MSc. Miguel A. Caicedo Baltodano - Fundación para el Estudio de Especies Invasivas (FuEDEI) & Consejo Nacional de Investigaciones Científicas y Técnicas (CONICET), Peru/Argentina.



Collaborators
Our work is empowered by an international network of researchers and institutional partners across the globe.

Global Collaboration Network
